Rust Basics.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for Rust Basics.

lessons.title: Hello Rust
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Your First Rust Program
1. Write a `main` function
2. Use the `println!` macro to print your name
3. Print your favorite programming language on the next line
lessons.rubric: Code uses fn and println! correctly.

lessons.title: Variables & Mutability
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Mutable Variables
1. Declare an immutable variable `name`
2. Declare a **mutable** variable `score` starting at 0
3. Add 10 to the score
4. Print both
lessons.rubric: Score declared as mut and modified correctly.

lessons.title: Data Types
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Working with Types
1. Create a boolean `is_learning`
2. Create a tuple `coords` with two integers (x, y)
3. Extract x and y from the tuple using dot notation (`coords.0`)
lessons.hint: bool type. .0 gets first tuple element, .1 gets second.
lessons.rubric: Correct types and tuple extraction used.

lessons.title: Functions in Rust
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Write a Function
1. Write a function `is_even(n: i32) -> bool`
2. Return true if n is even, false otherwise
3. Use implicit return (no semicolon on the last line)
4. Test it in main
lessons.hint: i32 and bool types. == for comparison.
lessons.rubric: Function signature correct. Implicit return works.

lessons.title: Control Flow
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Loop and Branch
1. Loop from 1 to 10 (inclusive, use `1..=10`)
2. Print "Even" or "Odd" for each number using an `if/else`
lessons.hint: 1..=10 for inclusive range. else for the alternative branch.
lessons.rubric: Loop runs 1 to 10. Correctly identifies even and odd.

Ownership & Borrowing.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for Ownership & Borrowing.

lessons.title: The Ownership Rules
lessons.theory:
## Memory Safety without Garbage Collection
Rust's most unique feature is its ownership system.

1. Each value has a variable called its **owner**.
2. There can only be **one owner** at a time.
3. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is **dropped** (memory freed).

```rust
let s1 = String::from("hello");
let s2 = s1; // s1 is MOVED to s2. s1 is no longer valid!

// println!("{}", s1); // ERROR: value borrowed here after move
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Understand Moves
1. Create a String `s1`
2. "Move" it to `s2` (assign s1 to s2)
3. To keep `s1` valid, you must **clone** it instead: `let s2 = s1.clone();`
4. Fix the code to use clone so both print statements work
lessons.hint: .clone() creates a deep copy so ownership isn't moved.
lessons.rubric: Code compiles and prints both strings by using clone().

lessons.title: Borrowing (References)
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Pass by Reference
1. Create a function `print_length` that takes a reference to a String (`&String`)
2. Pass a String to it from main using `&`
3. Print the string again in main to prove you didn't lose ownership
lessons.hint: &String for the type. &text to pass the reference.
lessons.rubric: Function takes &String. Main passes &text. Ownership is preserved.

lessons.title: Mutable References
lessons.theory:
## Changing Borrowed Data
You can modify borrowed data if you use a **mutable reference** (`&mut`).

**The Big Rule:** At any given time, you can have either:
- ONE mutable reference (`&mut T`)
- OR ANY NUMBER of immutable references (`&T`)

This prevents data races at compile time!
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Mutable Borrow
1. Create a mutable String
2. Create a function `append_world(s: &mut String)`
3. Use `s.push_str(" World")` inside the function
4. Pass a mutable reference to the function
lessons.hint: &mut String for the type. let mut for the variable. &mut greeting to pass.
lessons.rubric: Code correctly modifies the string via mutable reference.
